questions.question: Having flown 37 NM of your 83 NM cross-country flight, you determine that you are 4.5 KM left of course. If you turn 9° right, how far off course will you be at your destination?

After 83 nm of your 178 nm flight, you notice you are 2.4 nm left of course. If you have been flying a compass heading of 028°, which heading should you fly to intercept your original course at the destination?

You are coming home on your long solo cross-country flight while working towards your Private Pilot certificate. You end up flying over an airport that is 3.4 nautical miles left of your intended course. If you have covered 48% of the 153 NM flight, what correction angle must you turn to arrive on course 10 nautical miles prior to your destination?
During the outbound leg, you drift 4.7 statute miles off course to the left. What heading do you turn to in order to fly directly back to where you departed?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 36-40. Fuel on board: 74 gallons usable. Outbound true course: 340°. Cruise: 148 KTAS, 45.4 liters per hour. Variation: 9°E. Deviation: +3°. [[TABLE]]

questions.question: You are on a night cross-country heading to Smalltown Municipal Airport. The only airplane available tonight is the old one. The one without the GPS. The only avionics are an old VOR and an ILS receiver. It's ok though, because you know how to dead reckon and you have a new sectional downloaded on your EFB. En route to your first checkpoint you notice that you are a few miles right of course, 3.7 SM to be exact. You have flown 27 NM of 118 NM. You have been flying heading 128° so far. What heading do you fly to proceed directly to your destination?

You're enjoying great evening flying weather and have just embarked on the final leg of a VFR cross-country flight. It's golden hour and while enjoying the views and long shadows below you realize you drifted off course. The final leg is a total of 98 nm. According to your recently discovered actual position, you have covered 31 nm. You are 4.2 nm right of course and you want to re-intercept your plotted course 15 nm from your destination. How many degrees do you need to turn?
After flying 57 nm of your 192 nm flight, you notice that you are 3 nm left of course. If you turn 7° right, how far will you be from the destination when you rejoin your course?
You realize after flying 34 SM that you need additional wind correction of 15° to maintain your original planned course. How many statute miles have you drifted off course?

You are on a 320 SM cross-country flight. You notice that you seem to be a bit off course, and after locating yourself you find you are 4.3 NM off course just a quarter of the way into your flight. How many degrees must you turn to arrive on course 10 NM before your destination?

You depart on your planned heading of 329° and ground speed of 109 KTS; your trip is 176 nm long. After 24 minutes, you notice that you are 2.7 sm left of course. What heading will take you directly to your destination?

After flying 113 nm of your 432 nm flight, you check your position and see that you have drifted 11.5° left of course. If you continue on your current track, how far from your destination will you be when you arrive?
What heading correction should you make to fly directly to the destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 14: After flying 113 nm of your 432 nm flight, you check your position and see that you have drifted 11.5° left of course. If you continue on your current track, how far from your destination will you be when you arrive?

questions.question: You are currently 118 NM into your 300 SM trip. You decide to do a position check and determine that you are currently 3.6 SM off course. How many degrees do you need to turn to proceed direct to your destination?
Twenty-five minutes into your trip, you realize you are 5.6 NM off course. Your GS is 97 knots. How many degrees correction do you need to parallel your course?
Referencing the previous question, you want to re-intercept your course 45 NM prior to your destination that is 107 NM from present position. How many additional degrees are needed to do this?
questions.context: Referenced question 23: Twenty-five minutes into your trip, you realize you are 5.6 NM off course. Your GS is 97 knots. How many degrees correction do you need to parallel your course?

questions.question: After flying 113 NM of your 186 NM flight, you check your position and see that you have drifted 3.5° left of course. If you continue on your current track, how far from your destination will you be when you arrive?
In the previous scenario, what heading correction should you make to fly directly to the destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 9: After flying 113 NM of your 186 NM flight, you check your position and see that you have drifted 3.5° left of course. If you continue on your current track, how far from your destination will you be when you arrive?

questions.question: On your long cross-country flight for your private pilot license, you determine you have drifted 5 nautical miles south of your desired course. If you have 82 nautical miles remaining on your 133 nautical mile flight, how many degrees must you turn to regain your course 10 nautical miles before your destination?

Unfortunately, your navigation system seems to be malfunctioning, because at your 50 miles you find yourself 3 miles off left of course. If your original heading was 240° what heading would you need to fly to parallel your intended course?
If you still have 90 miles left to fly what total heading correction is required to fly directly to your destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 38: Unfortunately, your navigation system seems to be malfunctioning, because at your 50 miles you find yourself 3 miles off left of course. If your original heading was 240° what heading would you need to fly to parallel your intended course?

questions.question: You are flying a heading of 120° along an airway that is 75 nm long when ATC calls up and says they are showing you 3 nm off course to the left. If you have flown 45 nm how many degrees off course are you?
From the previous question, if you want to get back on course in the next 5 nm what heading should you fly?
questions.context: Referenced question 9: You are flying a heading of 120° along an airway that is 75 nm long when ATC calls up and says they are showing you 3 nm off course to the left. If you have flown 45 nm how many degrees off course are you?

questions.question: After flying 21 minutes of your 113 nm flight, you calculate your ground speed as 123 kts, and notice that you are 3 miles south of course. If your original heading was 284°, what heading should you fly to go directly to the destination?
You depart on a heading of 325° but 45 NM into your 85 NM trip you find yourself 3 NM to the right of your intended course. How many degrees off course have you drifted?
What heading do you need to intercept your course 10 NM prior to your destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 12: You depart on a heading of 325° but 45 NM into your 85 NM trip you find yourself 3 NM to the right of your intended course. How many degrees off course have you drifted?

questions.question: You find yourself 7.4 SM off course after flying 128 KM of your 207 NM flight. How much of a course correction will you need to make to make it to your destination?

While on your cross country you realize you have drifted 3 NM off course to the right. If you have flown 45 NM using your true course of 163° how many degrees do you need to turn to parallel your course?
Assuming you made your heading correction from above, if you are 20 NM from your destination what heading should you fly?
questions.context: Referenced question 23: While on your cross country you realize you have drifted 3 NM off course to the right. If you have flown 45 NM using your true course of 163° how many degrees do you need to turn to parallel your course?

questions.question: Shortly after takeoff on a cross-country flight you notice a new ethanol plant being constructed. As you gawk at the plant, your eyes are off the instruments for a while. When you look back you are 4.3 miles off course and had only traveled 20 miles. If the total distance remaining to your destination is 153 miles, how many degrees must you turn to parallel your course?
As you are tracking inbound of the VXV VOR, your needle shows you 4° off course to the left. If you are at 142 nm from the station, how many miles off course are you?
You notice that you are 4 NM off course after flying for 30 NM. How many degrees would you need to turn to parallel your original course?
From the previous question, if you have 68 NM remaining how many additional degrees would you need to turn if you wanted to intercept your original course 20 NM before your destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 12: You notice that you are 4 NM off course after flying for 30 NM. How many degrees would you need to turn to parallel your original course?

questions.question: If you have traveled 58 miles and are 3 miles off course, what is the course change required to parallel the original desired course?

You are out for a short afternoon practice flight for the VFR Navigation event. Unfortunately, the government seems to be testing GPS jamming equipment today and you have lost the magenta line as your GPS failed. You find yourself reverting to pilotage once again, and discover you have drifted 1.5 nautical miles left of your course. If you discover this 12.1 nautical miles into a 23.3 nautical mile leg, how many degrees must you turn to arrive over the checkpoint?
After flying 48 nm of your 96 nm trip you realize you are 6.4 nm left of your intended course of 210°. What heading do you need to fly in order to parallel your desired course?
How much more do you need to turn if you would like to intercept your course at the VFR reporting point 15 nm prior to your destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 36: After flying 48 nm of your 96 nm trip you realize you are 6.4 nm left of your intended course of 210°. What heading do you need to fly in order to parallel your desired course?

questions.question: Referring to the previous question, if you wish to rejoin your intended course in 24 nm from your current position, what must your total heading change be from your original heading?
questions.context: Referenced question 1: After trying your hand at dead reckoning, you peek at your GPS and notice that you are 6 nm off course. If you were flying for 32 minutes at an average ground speed of 140 mph, how many degrees must you adjust your heading to parallel your original intended course?

questions.question: Being a proactive pilot you also decide to calculate how much you will have to turn back to the right after crossing one nautical mile to the left of the TFR so that you can proceed directly to your destination. How many degrees will you need to turn after passing the TFR?
questions.context: In the preflight briefing for your upcoming 328 statute mile cross country trip you learn that there will be a TFR directly under your true course of 332 degrees. The center of the TFR will be located 185 nautical miles along your route. The TFR is 5 nautical miles across, from the surface to 7,000 feet, and will be active from 1400Z to 1600Z. Use this information to answer the next 7 questions.

questions.question: You have been flying for 83 minutes on a true course of 250° indicating 97 knots on your way home from Oshkosh when you look down and notice you have drifted 6 NM north of your course. If you have 72 NM left to get home, how much will you have to turn to arrive directly over your home field? It is 73° at your cruising altitude of 4,500' MSL and the winds are 160° true at 22 knots.

You're flying your incomparable Cessna 150 around. It has a VOR receiver in it! Now you've been flying for 47 minutes at 100 kts on your 125 nm flight. Your VOR indicates you are 3° right of your course. How many miles are you off your course?
You fly for 87 minutes at a speed of 159 mph and find yourself 9 nm off course. If you have another 133 nm to go, what is your correction to parallel?
In the above question, what is the total correction to your destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 1: You fly for 87 minutes at a speed of 159 mph and find yourself 9 nm off course. If you have another 133 nm to go, what is your correction to parallel?

questions.question: After flying for 1 hour 22 minutes, you realize you have drifted 4 miles off course. If your ground speed was 133 knots, how many degrees must you turn to arrive directly over your destination which is only 25 minutes away?
(Referring Question #14) If the pilot wishes to rejoin the original course in 50 NM, what is the new heading to rejoin the course?
questions.context: Referenced question 14: A flight departs Los Angeles International (LAX) to Medford, OR (MFR), 448 NM apart. After flying 200 NM, this pilot discovers that the aircraft is 19.8 NM right of course. If the original heading is 003°, what is the new heading to parallel your course?

questions.question: After traveling 96 NM, the aircraft is 6.3 NM left of course. What is the total correction needed to return on course in 20 NM?

On a flight between two airports 200 NM apart and on a heading of 120°, there is a ridgeline exactly halfway between the two airports, which is perpendicular to the course. The pilot wishes to deviate to the left at a 45° angle, to intercept the ridgeline. The pilot wishes to deviate 13.3 NM before the ridgeline. Assuming no wind. If the pilot then wishes to re- intercept the course 50 NM immediately after the ridgeline, what is the new heading to intercept the original course?

You have flown for 22 minutes and the DME indicates that you've traveled 81 nm. You notice that you're 14 nm to the right of you intended course. If you have another 129 nm to your destination, what would be your new heading to your destination if your original heading was 062°?

You notice you have been flying 44 minutes now with a groundspeed of 117 kts and you are 13 nm off course. With 49 nm to your destination, what is the total correction needed to converge with you course at your destination?

You are directly flying from airport A to airport B, a distance of 138 statute miles with a magnetic course of 263°. Holding a constant headi ng, you note you are 4 miles north of course after flying 44 sm. What heading correction is needed to fly directly to B, assuming a constant wind?

The distance from airport A to airport B is 175 nautical miles. You have flown 102 nautical miles and notice the VOR indicates you are 9.5° right of course. You want to intercept the original course 22.5 nautical miles short of the destination. The original magnetic heading was 010° the new magnetic heading will be? ____________________________ (tenths) 2009 Regional Computer Accuracy Exam

You decide to test your pilotage and dead reckoning skills by renting a good old C172 minus the high-tech glass cockpit. 30 nm into your 170 nm flight, you realize your skills might be a little rusty as you determine you are 4 nm off course. How many degrees of total correction should you make to get yourself back on course?

You are flying VFR, and you crossed directly over your last checkpoint, right on course. But you are not so lucky on the next one; you are off 6 nm east of your intended course. You've been flying at a TAS of 130 kts for 45 minutes. The wind is 230° true at 28 kts, you have a true course of 190°, and the magnetic variation in the area is 8° east. If your next checkpoint is 34 nm away, how many degrees do you need to correct to fly over it?
After flying for 37 minutes on a compass heading of 115° you find yourself 13 km off course to the right. If your groundspeed is 138 knots, what CH must you fly to parallel your intended course?

There you were on a heading of 095° when you realize that you are 11 NM off course to the right. If you have traveled 68 NM up to this point, how many degrees must you turn to parallel your intended course?
In the previous problem, if you have 195 km left to destination, how many total degrees would you have to turn to intercept the original course 15 SM before your destination?
questions.context: Referenced question 1: There you were on a heading of 095° when you realize that you are 11 NM off course to the right. If you have traveled 68 NM up to this point, how many degrees must you turn to parallel your intended course?

questions.question: How far will John miss Mt. Adams by?

choices.text: He will only miss it by 1.17 nautical miles
He will miss the mountain by 11.7 nautical miles
He will miss the mountain by 7.0 nautical miles
He will only miss it by 3.10 nautical miles

questions.correctChoice: He will miss the mountain by 11.7 nautical miles
questions.context: John wants to fly directly from The Dalles, OR to Seattle, WA. However, the only problem is that there is a 12,000 foot mountain the way, Mt. Adams. There are calm winds aloft. He plans on flying the 118 nautical mile, true course of 324° route as follows: [[NEXT_LINE]] 1st Fly 20 nautical miles on the original true course. [[NEXT_LINE]] 2nd Fly on a true course of 310° for 93 km. This puts you abeam Mt. Adams. [[NEXT_LINE]] 3rd Fly on a true course of 345° to regain his original course.

questions.question: Will he rejoin his course prior to getting to Seattle? If so how far from Seattle will he rejoin his course?

choices.text: No, he will need at least a correction to a true heading of 356°
Yes, he will rejoin his course 14 nautical miles from Seattle
Yes, he will rejoin his course 20 nautical miles from Seattle
Yes, he will rejoin his course 7 nautical miles from Seattle

questions.correctChoice: Yes, he will rejoin his course 14 nautical miles from Seattle
questions.context: John wants to fly directly from The Dalles, OR to Seattle, WA. However, the only problem is that there is a 12,000 foot mountain the way, Mt. Adams. There are calm winds aloft. He plans on flying the 118 nautical mile, true course of 324° route as follows: [[NEXT_LINE]] 1st Fly 20 nautical miles on the original true course. [[NEXT_LINE]] 2nd Fly on a true course of 310° for 93 km. This puts you abeam Mt. Adams. [[NEXT_LINE]] 3rd Fly on a true course of 345° to regain his original course.

questions.question: John travels 202 kilometers on a True Heading of 132° and finds that he is 4.3 nautical miles off course to the left. Assuming calm winds aloft, what true heading does John need to parallel his original course?
In question 13, if John has 318 km left in his flight, but he wants to regain his original course with 25 nautical miles left, what will his true heading have to be?
questions.context: Referenced question 13: John travels 202 kilometers on a True Heading of 132° and finds that he is 4.3 nautical miles off course to the left. Assuming calm winds aloft, what true heading does John need to parallel his original course?

questions.question: After traveling 42.7 statute miles, Tina notices she is 9.1 kilometers right of course. If she has 101.1 nautical miles until her arrival point and her current true heading is 101°, what must Tina turn her true heading to in order to re-intercept her original course 20 nautical miles prior to her arrival point?
JoAnn is 4° right of course after 42 minutes and 30 seconds into her flight. If JoAnn's groundspeed is 126 mph, how many nautical miles is she off course by?

Traveling between Yakima, WA and Walla Walla, WA (distance 84 nautical miles) with a true heading of 100°, you realize you are 4.2 nautical miles to the south of course 1/3 of the way through the trip. What will be the new true heading to fly directly to Walla Walla?
